title: The simplest possible condition syntax should be preferred
description: The simplest possible condition syntax should be usedAnalyze your codeintentionality-clearmaintainabilityCode SmellQuick FixIDE quick fixes available with SonarQube for IDEclumsyWhy is this an issue?In the interests of keeping code clean, the simplest possible conditional syntax should be used. That meansusing the??=operator for a self-assign-if-not-null operation,using the??operator for an assign-if-not-null operation, andusing the ternary operator?:for assignment to a single variable.Noncompliant code exampleobject a = null, b = null, x; if (a != null) // Noncompliant; needlessly verbose { x = a; } else { x = b; } x = a != null ? a : b; // Noncompliant; better but could still be simplified x = (a == null) ? new object() : a; // Noncompliant if (condition) // Noncompliant { x = a; } else
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

noncompliant: x = a != null ? a : b; // Noncompliant - could use ?? operator
x = (a == null) ? new object() : a; // Noncompliant
compliant: x = a ?? b; // Compliant - uses null-coalescing operator
a ??= b; // Compliant - uses null-coalescing assignment
x = condition ? a : b; // Compliant - ternary for non-null checks

checks.message: Use null-coalescing operator (??) instead of verbose ternary expression for null checks

logic.detection_conditions: Ternary expression checking for null that could be simplified with ?? or ??= operators
logic.triggering_conditions: source contains patterns like 'a != null ? a : b' or '(a == null) ? b : a' that could use ??
logic.reasoning: The null-coalescing operator (??) and null-coalescing assignment (??=) are more concise and readable than verbose if-statements or ternary expressions for null checks.
